social_organic.twitter: MTHFR variants affect how your body processes folate. Standard panels often miss them. Whole genome sequencing reads every base pair — including the ones a targeted test skips. #MTHFR #Genetics
social_organic.linkedin:
If you carry an MTHFR variant, your body may process folate differently than the population average. This affects methylation — a biochemical process involved in DNA repair, cardiovascular health, and neurological function.

The challenge: most standard lab panels test for only one or two MTHFR variants. There are more.

Whole genome sequencing reads 100% of your DNA — not a curated slice of it. That means variants your doctor's standard panel wasn't designed to find are still in the data, still available for clinical review, still actionable.

If MTHFR runs in your family, or if you've been told your results are normal while symptoms persist, the gap between what standard testing covers and what your genome contains may be where your answer is hiding.

Whole genome sequencing doesn't replace your physician. It gives them a more complete picture to work with.

reddit.title: MTHFR gene variants and diet — what whole genome sequencing actually tells you that a standard panel might not
reddit.body:
There's a lot of noise online about MTHFR — some of it useful, a lot of it overstated. I want to share what the genomic science actually says, and where standard testing tends to fall short.

MTHFR (methylenetetrahydrofolate reductase) encodes an enzyme that converts folate into its active form, 5-methyltetrahydrofolate. This active form is essential for the methylation cycle — which touches DNA repair, homocysteine metabolism, and neurotransmitter synthesis.

The two most commonly tested variants are C677T and A1298C. Most standard genetic panels and some consumer tests check for these two. That's it.

The problem: those aren't the only clinically relevant variants in the MTHFR gene. And the dietary and supplementation implications can differ depending on which variant you actually carry, in which combination, and in what zygosity (one copy vs. two).

Whole genome sequencing reads the entire gene — not just the two positions a targeted panel was designed to interrogate. That distinction matters if your standard results came back 'normal' but you're still experiencing symptoms associated with impaired methylation: elevated homocysteine, certain B vitamin metabolism irregularities, or a family history of related cardiovascular or neurological conditions.

From a dietary standpoint, MTHFR variants are often discussed in terms of:
- Folate form (food-based folate vs. synthetic folic acid vs. methylfolate)
- B12 status and form (methylcobalamin vs. cyanocobalamin)
- Choline intake as an alternative methyl donor pathway

None of this is medical advice — and anyone acting on MTHFR findings should be doing so with a physician or registered dietitian who understands the methylation literature.

But the starting point is knowing what you actually carry. A two-variant panel gives you a partial answer. The full genome gives you the complete picture.

Happy to discuss the science if anyone has questions.

social_organic.facebook:
You may have heard about the MTHFR gene — it comes up a lot in conversations about nutrition, folate, and family health history. Here is what it actually means.

MTHFR is a gene that helps your body convert folate from food into the form it can actually use. Certain variants in this gene can affect that conversion process — which in turn touches things like cardiovascular health, homocysteine levels, and how your body responds to certain B vitamins.

The part that surprises many families: most standard tests only check for one or two MTHFR variants. There are more. And the dietary picture — which forms of folate and B12 are most useful for you specifically — can depend on exactly which variants you carry.

If MTHFR-related conditions run in your family, or if someone in your family has been told their results are 'normal' but something still feels incomplete, it may be worth asking whether a more complete genetic picture is available.

Whole genome sequencing reads 100% of the genome — including every position in the MTHFR gene, not just the two a targeted panel was designed to find. It does not replace a conversation with your doctor or a registered dietitian. But it gives them more complete information to work with.

If this resonates with your family's experience, we are glad to answer questions in the comments.
